title: Reproducibility rules in economics
text: In this two-part workshop, The first session covers foundational practices such as data management, version control, code organization, and documentation standards aligned with leading journals, helping students structure empirical workflows that enhance credibility and long-term impact. The second session builds on these foundations with hands-on applications and more advanced workflow strategies. Topics include preparing replication packages, improving computational efficiency, collaborative coding practices, and meeting journal data requirements. Together, the sessions provide practical tools to help students ensure their research meets evolving standards in applied economics.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Economics
text:
Reproducibility vs Replicability (@Cacioppo.etal2015)






Definitions: Reproducibility



A research study is reproducible if anybody (including the author of the study) can generate exactly the same results by using the same materials (e.g., data, code, and documentation) and procedures used in the study.








Definitions: Replicability



A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).


We will focus on reproducibility in this workshop.

Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials as were used by the original investigator.








Definitions: Replicability



A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).

section: How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?
text:
How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?






Rule



Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Economics
text:
Characteristics of high-quality reproducible research

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code
Workflows are automated through clearly annotated programs, making it easier for others to understand the replication process and improving the transparency of the research.
Comprehensive Documentation
Documentation of the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps saves time and enhances clarity for others who want to reproduce the research.

Reproducibility benefits you and your team members as well as the scientific community.

A project can be considered reproducible if the minimum requirements are met. However, it is still low-quality if reproducing it requires excessive time or resources. A high-quality reproducible project, on the other hand, demonstrates the following characteristics:

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
Reproducibility and Replicability
Here are the definitions of reproducibility and replicability from Cacioppo et al. (2015):



Definitions: Reproducibility


“Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials as were used by the original investigator.”







Definitions: Replicability


A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).




In essence, reproducibility asks:
“Can another researcher go from raw data to final results using only the materials you provide?”

So, what should we do to make our research reproducible?


Reproducing research involves using the original data and code, while replicating research involves new data collection and similar methods used in previous studies, the report says. Even when a study was rigorously conducted according to best practices, correctly analyzed, and transparently reported, it may fail to be replicated. (https://phys.org/news/2019-05-replicability-science.html)

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
section: How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?
text:
How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?



Minimum Rule


Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.




The following characteristics can further enhance the quality of reproducible research:


(i) Organized Project Folder


(ii) Streamlined Code 

(iii) Comprehensive Documentation 



Even if a project meets the minimum requirements for reproducibility, it may still be considered low-quality if reproducing it requires excessive time or resources. On the other hand, a high-quality reproducible project demonstrates the following characteristics

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code

Workflows are automated through clearly annotated programs, making it easier for others to understand the replication process and improving the transparency of the research.
less manual edits, copy-past steps, ad hoc file handling, etc. that can be error-prone and time-consuming.

Comprehensive Documentation

Documentation of the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps saves time and enhances clarity for others who want to reproduce the research.
The documentation should provide setup instructions, dependencies, and execution order.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text: Why reproducibility matters?

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
Benefits of reproducibility
Reproducibility practices (e.g., organized project folder, streamlined code, and comprehensive documentation) benefit you and your team members as well as the scientific community.

For you and your team:

Faster debugging and iteration

Easier collaboration and handoffs

Reduced risk of errors

For the scientific community:

Transparent and verifiable results

Lower replication costs

Greater credibility of research findings

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
Key Components of Reproducible Research


BasicsFile organizationStreamlined codeComprehensive documentation





Minimum Rule


Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.



 To achieve high-quality reproducibility in practice, the following components are essential:


(i) Organized Project Folder


(ii) Streamlined Code 

(iii) Comprehensive Documentation 



Place code files, data files, and documentation files in the right folders with clear names
For example

Data/Raw folder should contain the original data files, and Data/Processed folder should contain the cleaned data files.




Automate









The minimum rule ensures that a project is reproducible in principle. However, this alone does not guarantee that the research is easy to understand, verify, or reuse. High-quality reproducibility requires additional structure and clarity.
Organizing the project folder is the easiest one to implement, and you can do this from today.
The third component, which is comprehensive documentation, is also relatively easy to implement. You can start by writing a README file that explains the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps. For example, you can look at the README file of the Justin’s github repository.
The second component, which is streamlined code, may require more time and effort to implement. This is the focus of this workshop, and I’m going to introduce some tools and strategies to help you achieve this.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
What is Reproducibility?



Definition: Reproducibility


Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials as were used by the original investigator(Cacioppo et al. 2015).





In essence, reproducibility asks:
“Can another researcher go from raw data to final results using only the materials you provide?”

So, what should we do to make our research reproducible?


Reproducing research involves using the original data and code, while replicating research involves new data collection and similar methods used in previous studies, the report says. Even when a study was rigorously conducted according to best practices, correctly analyzed, and transparently reported, it may fail to be replicated. (https://phys.org/news/2019-05-replicability-science.html)

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
section: Take a look at an example of a reproducible project
text:
Take a look at an example of a reproducible project
We will go through an example of a reproducible project to see how the key components of reproducibility are implemented in practice.
Download the project material from here




NOTE


This material is based on the github repository of Prof. Taro Mieno (here), which is created to demonstrate the principles of reproducibility in scientific research in his course at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln. I modified the original material to fit the purpose of this workshop, but the core structure and content are based on his work.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
section: Growing Interest in Enhancing Transparency and Reproducibility in Academia
text:
Growing Interest in Enhancing Transparency and Reproducibility in Academia

Reproducibility has always been a core scientific principle. However, it has become a growing concern in modern research.
Why now?

Increasingly complex data and code pipelines (National Academies of Sciences et al. 2019)
Hidden steps and undocumented decisions (Gentzkow and Shapiro 2014)
Incentives favor speed over transparency (Franco et al. 2014)

Implications

Results are hard to verify
Reproducibility often fails even when data and code are available (Chang and Li 2018)

Credibility of research is at risk (Baker 2016)

Takeaway

Reproducibility practices are no longer optional—they are essential.



Reproducibility is not a new concern. It is a longstanding norm of science.
The reason might be because that research became more computationally complex.
AEA: Data and Code Availability Policy (https://www.aeaweb.org/journals/data/data-code-policy?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
section: Growing Interest in Enhancing Transparency and Reproducibility in Academia - Part 2
text:
Growing Interest in Enhancing Transparency and Reproducibility in Academia - Part 2
Reproducibility has always been a core scientific principle. However, it has become a growing concern in modern research.
Why now? - Increasingly complex data and code pipelines (National Academies of Sciences et al. 2019) - Hidden steps and undocumented decisions (Gentzkow and Shapiro 2014) - Incentives favor speed over transparency (Franco, Malhotra, and Simonovits 2014)
Implications - Results are hard to verify (National Academies of Sciences et al. 2019)
- Replication is costly or infeasible (Chang and Li 2018)
- Credibility of research is at risk (Baker 2016)
Takeaway + Reproducibility practices are no longer optional—they are essential.


Reproducibility is not a new concern. It is a longstanding norm of science.
The reason might be because that research became more computationally complex.
AEA: Data and Code Availability Policy (https://www.aeaweb.org/journals/data/data-code-policy?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
References




Baker, Monya. 2016. 1,500 Scientists Lift the Lid on Reproducibility.


Cacioppo, John T, Robert M Kaplan, Jon A Krosnick, James L Olds, and Heather Dean. 2015. “Social, Behavioral, and Economic Sciences Perspectives on Robust and Reliable Science.” Report of the Subcommittee on Replicability in Science Advisory Committee to the National Science Foundation Directorate for Social, Behavioral, and Economic Sciences 1: 795.


Chang, Andrew C, and Phillip Li. 2018. “Is Economics Research Replicable? Sixty Published Papers from Thirteen Journals Say ‘Often Not’.” Critical Finance Review 7: 1–25.


Franco, Annie, Neil Malhotra, and Gabor Simonovits. 2014. “Publication Bias in the Social Sciences: Unlocking the File Drawer.” Science 345 (6203): 1502–5.


Gentzkow, Matthew, and Jesse M Shapiro. 2014. Code and Data for the Social Sciences: A Practitioner’s Guide. Working Paper, University of Chicago.


National Academies of Sciences, Medicine, Policy, et al. 2019. Reproducibility and Replicability in Science. National Academies Press.
